2: Food production poses a major global sustainability challenge for the 21st century. According to the European Commission

(EurActive, 2009), the food and drink sector contributes 23% of global resource use, 18% of greenhouse gas emissions and 31% of acidifying emissions. The World Bank projects demand for food to increase by 50% by 2050, and demand for meat by 85%, mainly as emerging economies like China and India become richer and adopt Western-style eating habits, rich in meat and dairy products. In Australia, the average consumption of poultry meat has increased
from 10.5 kg/person in 1969 to 37.2 kg/person in 2009 (ABARES, 2010), with approximately 470 million meat chickens (also known as broilers) processed in 2009 (DAFF, 2011).

There is an increased recognition and acceptance of the need for 'systems' perspective to develop more sustainable systems and societies within the agriculture and food industry (Notarnicola et al., 2012). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is widely considered to be a leading holistic method to evaluate the environmental impact during the life cycle of products. LCA have been used widely to assess the environmental impact of chicken meat in Europe (e.g. Ahlmén et al., 2001; Baumgartner et al., 2008; Boggia et al., 2010; Katajajuuri, 2007; LCA Food, 2003; and Williams et al., 2006), Brazil (Freitas de Alvarenga et al., 2012), and USA (Pelletier, 2008) - each study with its unique scope and method- ological approach to suit their respective aims and objectives, which is commonplace for agricultural LCA (Harris and Narayanaswamy, 2009). Other LCA studies focus on specific life cycle stages such as broiler feed production (Tongpoola et al., in press; Nguyen et al., 2012), poultry slaughterhouse water use (Kist al., 2009), and post-farm stages (Davis and Sonesson, 2008).

At the same time there is a need to improve on methodological harmonization in order to more effectively support future devel- opments and applications of LCA (Fantin et al., 2012; Finnveden, 2000; Notarnicola et al., 2012; Peacock et al., 2011). Opportunities to increase the utility and application of the ISO LCA framework (ISO, 2006a,b) include to:

<list>Integrate and connect with other methods suitable to address the broad range of challenges facing the agriculture and food industry (Jeswani et al., 2010; Roy et al., 2009) such as expo- sures to pesticides, soil function impairment, biodiversity los- ses, and invasive GM crops (Notarnicola et al., 2012); and 
Establish regionally or nationally adapted and relevant meth- odologies, as argued by for example Ruvario et al. (2012) to suit Brazil's environmental and technological context for agricul- tural production and URS Australia Pty Ltd (2009) and BPIC (2010) for the Australian rural industries and construction sector respectively.</list>

Consistent methodology is arguably an essential requirement, especially for LCA used for comparative purposes. Key elements of a consistent methodology are the issues of allocation of impacts between co-products, which remains one of the most debated aspects, given its potentially large influence on final outcomes (Cederberg and Stadig, 2003; Cherubini et al., 2011; Flysjö et al., 2012; Reijnders and Huijbregts, 2005; Weidema, 2000), and choice of life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) methodologies (e.g. Eco-indicator 99 (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001), IMPACT 2002+ (Jolliet et al., 2003), ReCiPe (Goedkoop et al., 2009), and TRACI (US EPA, 2010)). The widespread use of several different LCIA methods has created confusion over which methodology to use and criticism when the use of LCA gives different results depending on the methodology chosen (European Commission - Joint Research Centre - Institute for Environment and Sustainability, 2010).

This paper presents part of the results of a study of Inghams Enterprises Pty Ltd's Australian chicken meat production, leaving out further processed products after Inghams' primary processing plants and retailers or quick service restaurants. Inghams Australian operations encompass a fully integrated farming, primary and further processing poultry business, including over 70 owned and operated facilities and over 160 contracted broiler farms with a combined production of close to 200 million birds in 2010/11. Inghams is one of two major integrated compa- nies supplying more than 70% of Australia's broiler chickens (ACMF, 2010).

The initial priorities for the study were to identify environ- mental hot spots with the view to reduce impacts and costs, and improve products, processes and supply chains for Inghams. Of specific interest to Inghams was the comparative environmental performance of conventional and free-range chicken production in terms of overall weighted environmental impact, non-renewable energy resource depletion, global warming impact, and water depletion. The study was conducted with a view to ensure consis- tency and comparability with state of the art LCA methodology in Australia.

The physical scope of the study is cradle to retailer or quick service restaurant gate, including: feed ingredient production, poultry grow-out, meat processing, product packaging, distribution and cold storage. The retail or quick service restaurant and consumption life cycle stages are not reported in this paper due to lack of data. It shall be noted that the retail and consumptions stage are likely very significant in terms of environmental impact and mitigation potential. Davis and Sonesson (2008) demonstrate that consumer actions can be just as important as industrial actions to reduce the environmental impact of a chicken meal, including less food waste in the household. Roughly one-third of food produced for human consumption is lost or wasted globally (Gustavsson et al., 2011). In general food waste is one of the least utilized materials, but with potential to be converted to replace conven- tional fossil fuels as well as utilized as soil fertility sustaining amendments (Notarnicola et al., 2012).

(EurActive, 2009), the food and drink sector contributes 23% of global resource use, 18% of greenhouse gas emissions and 31% of acidifying emissions. The World Bank projects demand for food to increase by 50% by 2050, and demand for meat by 85%, mainly as emerging economies like China and India become richer and adopt Western-style eating habits, rich in meat and dairy products. In Australia, the average consumption of poultry meat has increased
from 10.5 kg/person in 1969 to 37.2 kg/person in 2009 (ABARES, 2010), with approximately 470 million meat chickens (also known as broilers) processed in 2009 (DAFF, 2011).

There is an increased recognition and acceptance of the need for 'systems' perspective to develop more sustainable systems and societies within the agriculture and food industry (Notarnicola et al., 2012). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is widely considered to be a leading holistic method to evaluate the environmental impact during the life cycle of products. LCA have been used widely to assess the environmental impact of chicken meat in Europe (e.g. Ahlmén et al., 2001; Baumgartner et al., 2008; Boggia et al., 2010; Katajajuuri, 2007; LCA Food, 2003; and Williams et al., 2006), Brazil (Freitas de Alvarenga et al., 2012), and USA (Pelletier, 2008) - each study with its unique scope and method- ological approach to suit their respective aims and objectives, which is commonplace for agricultural LCA (Harris and Narayanaswamy, 2009). Other LCA studies focus on specific life cycle stages such as broiler feed production (Tongpoola et al., in press; Nguyen et al., 2012), poultry slaughterhouse water use (Kist al., 2009), and post-farm stages (Davis and Sonesson, 2008).

At the same time there is a need to improve on methodological harmonization in order to more effectively support future devel- opments and applications of LCA (Fantin et al., 2012; Finnveden, 2000; Notarnicola et al., 2012; Peacock et al., 2011). Opportunities to increase the utility and application of the ISO LCA framework (ISO, 2006a,b) include to:

<list>Integrate and connect with other methods suitable to address the broad range of challenges facing the agriculture and food industry (Jeswani et al., 2010; Roy et al., 2009) such as expo- sures to pesticides, soil function impairment, biodiversity los- ses, and invasive GM crops (Notarnicola et al., 2012); and 
Establish regionally or nationally adapted and relevant meth- odologies, as argued by for example Ruvario et al. (2012) to suit Brazil's environmental and technological context for agricul- tural production and URS Australia Pty Ltd (2009) and BPIC (2010) for the Australian rural industries and construction sector respectively.</list>

Consistent methodology is arguably an essential requirement, especially for LCA used for comparative purposes. Key elements of a consistent methodology are the issues of allocation of impacts between co-products, which remains one of the most debated aspects, given its potentially large influence on final outcomes (Cederberg and Stadig, 2003; Cherubini et al., 2011; Flysjö et al., 2012; Reijnders and Huijbregts, 2005; Weidema, 2000), and choice of life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) methodologies (e.g. Eco-indicator 99 (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001), IMPACT 2002+ (Jolliet et al., 2003), ReCiPe (Goedkoop et al., 2009), and TRACI (US EPA, 2010)). The widespread use of several different LCIA methods has created confusion over which methodology to use and criticism when the use of LCA gives different results depending on the methodology chosen (European Commission - Joint Research Centre - Institute for Environment and Sustainability, 2010).

This paper presents part of the results of a study of Inghams Enterprises Pty Ltd's Australian chicken meat production, leaving out further processed products after Inghams' primary processing plants and retailers or quick service restaurants. Inghams Australian operations encompass a fully integrated farming, primary and further processing poultry business, including over 70 owned and operated facilities and over 160 contracted broiler farms with a combined production of close to 200 million birds in 2010/11. Inghams is one of two major integrated compa- nies supplying more than 70% of Australia's broiler chickens (ACMF, 2010).

The initial priorities for the study were to identify environ- mental hot spots with the view to reduce impacts and costs, and improve products, processes and supply chains for Inghams. Of specific interest to Inghams was the comparative environmental performance of conventional and free-range chicken production in terms of overall weighted environmental impact, non-renewable energy resource depletion, global warming impact, and water depletion. The study was conducted with a view to ensure consis- tency and comparability with state of the art LCA methodology in Australia.

The physical scope of the study is cradle to retailer or quick service restaurant gate, including: feed ingredient production, poultry grow-out, meat processing, product packaging, distribution and cold storage. The retail or quick service restaurant and consumption life cycle stages are not reported in this paper due to lack of data. It shall be noted that the retail and consumptions stage are likely very significant in terms of environmental impact and mitigation potential. Davis and Sonesson (2008) demonstrate that consumer actions can be just as important as industrial actions to reduce the environmental impact of a chicken meal, including less food waste in the household. Roughly one-third of food produced for human consumption is lost or wasted globally (Gustavsson et al., 2011). In general food waste is one of the least utilized materials, but with potential to be converted to replace conven- tional fossil fuels as well as utilized as soil fertility sustaining amendments (Notarnicola et al., 2012).

8: The study was conducted in accordance with the recently launched Australian Building Product Life Cycle Inventory (BP LCI) methodology (BPIC, 2010). The primary aim of the BP LCI project is to provide a nationally consistent level playing field LCA method- ology for the building and construction sector, which allows for comparison of products and services across Australia. The BP LCI project was developed to be consistent with the goals of the broader Australian Life Cycle Inventory (AusLCI) database project (AusLCI, 2009). It was believed by the BP LCI project team that solving methodological problems to encompass the breadth of issues for construction materials and products should provide a methodology that is similarly consistent and applicable in most if not all other sectors (BPIC, 2010, p. 7).
9: Chicken production begins by importing selected eggs from overseas. The eggs are taken to a quarantine facility, and after hatching the day-old chicks (known as great grandparents) are taken to the rearing farm where they will be fed until ready to lay the eggs when they are taken to the breeder farm. Once they lay the eggs the process starts all over again till the chicks reach the fourth generation, which are the broiler chickens. These will go to the primary processing plant and, depending on the product, to the further processing plant before going to the supermarkets or retailers. The system included in the analysis is depicted in Fig. 1. The following sub-processes had to be cut off from the analysis because of lack of available data and/or their negligible importance:

1 A growing percentage of Inghams' production is free-range chicken. The term "free-range" indicates that chickens have access to paddocks during the day (weather permitting) and are free to roam, scratch and peck. Inghams' free-range chickens are herded into their sheds at night to prevent attack by natural preda- tors (Inghams Enterprises Pty Ltd, 2009). All feed is formulated to meet the inde- pendent body Free-Range Egg & Poultry Association (FREPA) standards. The free- range growing procedures are regulated by FREPA.

Farm cleaning chemicals production and use (assumed to be negligible based on established scoping quantities) 
Primary processing plant water treatment chemicals produc- tion and use (no data available, assumed small impact) 
Water evaporation and leakage (no data available, small envi- ronmental impact from evaporation although significant to mass balance)</list>

12: <header>2.2. Life cycle inventory data and functional unit </header>
The analysis was performed with the SimaPro software package version 7.2.4. Primary production data is based on 12 months of operation (financial year2 2010-11 or most recent available prior) from:
Inghams' internal production and sustainability reporting systems, and
Inghams' regulatory energy and greenhouse gas emission reporting

Secondary data was used to estimate on-farm air emissions based on the Australian Government's National Pollution Inventory (NPI, 2002), Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) guidelines and poultry research (Ritz et al., 2004; Katajajuuri, 2007; Gay, undated; and LCA Food, 2003):

For broiler farms LCA Food and Katajajuuri both use ammonia emission rates around 15 g/kg broiler. The emission rates in Ritz et al. and Gay translate to a range between 0 and 15 g/kg, assuming an average of 42 growing days to reach 2.5 kg live- weight, and the NPI recommend 7.7 g/kg. The NPI provides an approximate mid-point value and was therefore be used as the default emission factor.
For breeder hens the NPI emission factor of 598 g/yr for hens productive for more than six months.

Particular attention was directed to the production of feed ingredient (e.g. grains) as a major component of the burden asso- ciated with chicken production. Feed ingredient background data was selected to increase comparability with the Australian

Government's Rural Industries Research and Development Corpo- ration (RIRDC) commissioned LCAs. Other background data, including energy consumption, transport, packaging materials, waste management, was drawn primarily from the Australasian LCA Dataset, and ecoinvent and US LCI for imported products. All background processes were adapted to consistent economic allo- cation in accordance with the BP LCI and AusLCI methodology and guidelines.

The chosen functional unit for the study was 1 ton (t) of roast chicken and breast fillet from cradle to retail or quick food restau- rant gate for conventional and free-range production respectively. Table 2 lists the main direct inputs to the product system under study for production 1 kg of conventional fresh produce, organized under the following headings: feed production (based on FCR of 1.8 for liveweight broilers); hatcheries and egg import; breeder and rearing farms, broiler farms; primary and further processing plants; corporate operations and overheads; and distribution and cold storage.

In addition to the life cycle stages in Table 2 product packaging was modeled based on typical specification for eight bulk packed 1.50 kg chickens in 146 X 371 X 552 mm cartons (0.495 kg) and white LDPE liner (0.043 kg).

16: There is no differentiation between free-range and conventional chicken growth up until broiler production. Furthermore, there were no direct measurements available from contract farms growing free- range broilers. The following basic factors were used to differentiate free-range from conventional broiler production in the states Victoria, Queensland, Western Australia (WA) and Tasmania:

<list>The difference in Feed Conversion Ration (FCR) was be used to calculate the amount of feed required to grow the bird 1 kg in weight. 
The difference in broiler farm mortality rates was used to adjust feed consumed by broilers not used for meat production. The differences in mortality rates were halved based on the assumption that mortalities occur on average at half the final weight, although not taking into account that the feed consumption isn't linear over the growth of the chicken. 
The difference in days spent on broiler farms between free- range and conventional chicken was used to proportionally adjust broiler farm facility operations (e.g. heating and venti- lation). Free-range required between 2% and 6% longer time on farm per kg broiler compared to conventional growth.</list>

Differences from access to outdoor areas for free-range chicken have not been included in the study. Variations in feed composition for free-range and conventional feed compositions were according to Inghams' feed specialists negligible.

18: During the meat production stage, several economically valu- able by-products are produced besides the main meat products

such as whole chicken, breast fillets, quarter chickens etc. The approach to allocation for this study is based on the requirements and guidelines in leading national LCA and LCI methodologies (AusLCI, 2009, p. 20 and BPIC, 2010, p. 27). Both the AusLCI and BPIC approaches require economic allocation where allocation cannot be avoided by system expansion or subdividing systems or where it's not possible to establish such underlying physical relationships to differentiate the environmental burden of co-products.

In addition to allowing for consistent allocation up and down supply chains, economic allocation reflects the objective of the industry which is to make money, not mass or energy. The indus- try's operations and product mix will be optimized for economic return. Hence, the principle product by far is chicken meat for human consumption and should be attributed the largest propor- tion of the process burdens, and not the by-products such as feathers, blood, lungs, skins, trim, etc. with low economic value to society. By contrast, mass allocation would give about half of the burden to the principal meat products and half to the less valued by-products.

Although in practice Inghams' primary processing plants produce both free-range and conventional products, the meat processing of conventional and free-range reared broilers is iden- tical. In this way, allocation can be avoided for meat processing of free-range and conventional products in accordance with separa- tion of processes, in the hierarchy for allocation procedures proposed in the AusLCI guidelines (see AusLCI, 2009, p. 20).

The LCA was conducted on facility or enterprise level, estab- lishing an overall per ton chicken production impact. Allocation to primary processing meat products was based on the relative wholesale price of individual product categories compared with the average total fresh produce wholesale value. For conventional reared chicken, the allocation relative to average mass unit of produce was calculated to:

<list>-27.8% for roast chicken, and 
+94.3% for breast fillet.</list>

The relative price difference between roast chicken and breast fillet products from free-range grown broilers was different to that of conventional chicken, and the allocation relative to average mass unit of free-range produce was calculated to:

<list>-32.7% for free-range roast chicken, and 
+108.2% for free-range breast fillet</list>

It was not possible to establish the economic value of chicken litter for allocation purposes in this project. Although litter as a resource contains valuable nutrients for crop the value of the litter is very low comparison to the main chicken products and therefore the effects of this omission on the overall result is assumed to be small.

The indus- try's operations and product mix will be optimized for economic return. Hence, the principle product by far is chicken meat for human consumption and should be attributed the largest propor- tion of the process burdens, and not the by-products such as feathers, blood, lungs, skins, trim, etc. with low economic value to society. By contrast, mass allocation would give about half of the burden to the principal meat products and half to the less valued by-products.

Although in practice Inghams' primary processing plants produce both free-range and conventional products, the meat processing of conventional and free-range reared broilers is iden- tical. In this way, allocation can be avoided for meat processing of free-range and conventional products in accordance with separa- tion of processes, in the hierarchy for allocation procedures proposed in the AusLCI guidelines (see AusLCI, 2009, p. 20).

The LCA was conducted on facility or enterprise level, estab- lishing an overall per ton chicken production impact. Allocation to primary processing meat products was based on the relative wholesale price of individual product categories compared with the average total fresh produce wholesale value. For conventional reared chicken, the allocation relative to average mass unit of produce was calculated to:

<list>-27.8% for roast chicken, and 
+94.3% for breast fillet.</list>

The relative price difference between roast chicken and breast fillet products from free-range grown broilers was different to that of conventional chicken, and the allocation relative to average mass unit of free-range produce was calculated to:

<list>-32.7% for free-range roast chicken, and 
+108.2% for free-range breast fillet</list>

It was not possible to establish the economic value of chicken litter for allocation purposes in this project. Although litter as a resource contains valuable nutrients for crop the value of the litter is very low comparison to the main chicken products and therefore the effects of this omission on the overall result is assumed to be small.

25: 3.1. Impact assessment for the whole chicken product and after allocation between roast chicken and breast fillet

The results are presented in overall weighted environmental ecopoint impact, non-renewable energy resource depletion, global warming impact, and water depletion. Fig. 3 shows the calculated national average cradle to retail or quick food restaurant impact indicators for the sub-product systems: feed ingredient production; feed production plant operation; chicken grow-out farm operation; meat processing plants; product packaging; corporate operations and overheads; and distribution and cold storage (Fig. 4).

Feed ingredient production is the most significant life cycle stage in terms of water depletion (66%) and ecopoints (49%). The environmental impact intensity and profile vary significantly between ingredient types. The highest overall (ecopoint) contri- butions are from soy meal, grains and meatmeal, making up 79% of the impact from feed ingredients.

Wheat|49%|Canola seed|1% Sorghum|16%|Field Pea|<1% Soy meal|11%|Lupin meal|<1% Barley|7%|Oats|<0.5% Meat meal|5%|Lupin seed|<0.5% Canola meal (Hi Protein)|2%|Blood meal|<0.5% Cotton meal|2%|Full fat soymeal|<0.5% Millrun|2%|Poultry meal|<0.5% Canola meal (expeller)|1%|Sunflower meal|<0.5%

Poultry grow-out operation is the most significant life cycle stage in the impact categories non-renewable energy depletion (55%) and global warming (42%), primarily from gas and electricity consumption. In this life cycle stage, ammonia emissions, formed by bacterial decomposition of the fecal matter contribute 41% of the poultry grow-out ecopoint impact. The impacts from ammonia emissions are from acidification, eutrophication and respiratory effects.

Meat processing makes up 10-20% across the four impact categories. Close to 99% of the impacts are from energy consump- tion (electricity and gas), water use and wastewater output. Fig. 4 shows the calculated national average cradle to retail or quick food restaurant impact indicators for an alternative set of sub- product systems: feed production plants; hatcheries and egg import; breeder and rearing farms, broiler farms; meat processing plants; product packaging; corporate operations and overheads; and distribution and cold storage.

The broiler production, including the embodied impact from feed ingredient production, is by far the highest impact stage making up 67% (non-renewable energy consumption) to 72% (ecopoints) of the total impact life cycle impact. Broiler production, including feed ingredient impacts, combined with meat processing makes up 83-89% of the impact across the four categories.

Free-range broilers impacts at farm gate vary only between -0.3% and 1.2% compared to conventional production in the states included in this study. However, where broiler production impacts vary only insignificantly between free-range and conventional growth, allocation between end products is a more significant factor. The impact per ton of product type is shown in Table 3.

The results of this study can be interpreted and applied to reduce the environmental impact and improve efficiency across Inghams' product lines. This in-turn may contribute to the long-term economic success of Inghams by for example enhancing the brand with customers, retailers and staff for the corporation's environmental sustainability. By understanding the relative impacts of the various operations over which Inghams (as a producer) ultimately have control, it enables the company to take targeted initiatives and investments into procurement, energy and process efficiency measures, product design, packaging and logistics.

Upstream feed production (e.g. soy meal, grains, meatmeal) has the largest overall impact and water depleting part of the chicken meat supply chain. Therefore, productivity measures, such as feed conversion ratio (FCR) in broiler production and adoption of low-

impact agricultural practices by feed ingredient suppliers, that for example reduce the use of energy-intensive synthetic fertilizers and emissions resulting from their application, can significantly affect the environmental performance of chicken products. Energy use in poultry grow-out and meat processing are the main sources of greenhouse gas emissions and non-renewable energy depletion over the life cycle. This highlights the significance of shifting to clean renewable sources of energy and efforts to reduce the energy consumption of growing barns and in processing plants. Ammonia emissions from chicken litter contribute significantly to the overall life cycle impact, primarily from eutrophication, acidification and negative impact on human health (exposure to ammonia can irritate the respiratory tract and eyes). These impacts are included in the ecopoint metric but would not be recognized if the scope of the study is limited to a small set of impacts typically seen as key for the agricultural sector (e.g. global warming, water depletion, land use, toxicity or energy use). <list>The main impact overall is from consumption of chicken feed. There is very limited information available for the specific downstream production impacts of the feed ingredients used.

With the majority of the life cycle impact residing in Inghams' supply chain and from emissions not routinely measured or

monitored by Inghams (e.g. ammonia emissions from disposed or sold litter), it will be important to establish suitable mechanisms for sharing production data from contract broiler farm operations, feed ingredient production and manure management in order to increase the accuracy of the LCA results.

37: There is increasing interest from major retailers internation- ally and in Australia to introduce consumer-facing environmental information. This study provides the foundation for Inghams to contribute to the national and international development of LCA based environmental and sustainability based labeling of food products. Additional value can be added by extending the scope and further investigate how the use of Inghams' products by consumers, retailers and restaurants includes storage, cooking and waste generation affects the overall environmental footprint.

In general, the authors recommend reaching a wider consensus in the LCA community on consistent level playing field method- ology, specifically in terms of life cycle impact assessment and allocation. This is arguably the only way to enable a meaningful comparison between assessments, to avoid the possible misinter- pretation or misuse of the results themselves, and to increase the credibility and application of LCA. The approach used for this study builds on and demonstrates basic cross-sector application of the level playing field LCA methodology recently pioneered by the Australian building product sector.
38: The allocated impact per product was calculated to 11.4 and 12.4 ecopoints per ton of roast chicken and 35.2 and 33.4 ecopoints per

ton of breast fillet for free-range and conventional production respectively. The detailed life cycle analysis of Inghams' chicken production preformed here has shed light on which are the three environmental hot spots in the life cycle, namely feed ingredient production, broiler farm operation and meat processing plants. Feed ingredient production show significant improvement poten- tial; with environmental impact intensity ranging from 0.4 to 0.8 ecopoints per ton for wheat and grain co-product millrun to 8.4 ecopoints per ton from production of canola seed. The study has not successfully integrated and connected with other methods to address the broad range of challenges facing the agriculture and food industry such as exposures to pesticides, soil function impairment, biodiversity losses, and invasive GM crops. Several LCA studies of poultry production and products have been published. The most common unit for comparison is the calculated life-cycle global warming potential, typically assessed from cradle to farm gate or retail. Often sufficiently detailed information of the assumptions behind meat processing and allo- cation approach is missing in cradle to retail or consumer studies. A consistent and level playing field methodology must be established for the food sector in order for LCA to provide meaningful, reliable and comprehensive input into businesses decision-making. 44: The allocated impact per product was calculated to 11.4 and 12.4 ecopoints per ton of roast chicken and 35.2 and 33.4 ecopoints per

ton of breast fillet for free-range and conventional production respectively. The detailed life cycle analysis of Inghams' chicken production preformed here has shed light on which are the three environmental hot spots in the life cycle, namely feed ingredient production, broiler farm operation and meat processing plants. Feed ingredient production show significant improvement poten- tial; with environmental impact intensity ranging from 0.4 to 0.8 ecopoints per ton for wheat and grain co-product millrun to 8.4 ecopoints per ton from production of canola seed. The study has not successfully integrated and connected with other methods to address the broad range of challenges facing the agriculture and food industry such as exposures to pesticides, soil function impairment, biodiversity losses, and invasive GM crops. Several LCA studies of poultry production and products have been published. The most common unit for comparison is the calculated life-cycle global warming potential, typically assessed from cradle to farm gate or retail. Often sufficiently detailed information of the assumptions behind meat processing and allo- cation approach is missing in cradle to retail or consumer studies. A consistent and level playing field methodology must be established for the food sector in order for LCA to provide meaningful, reliable and comprehensive input into businesses decision-making. 49: The allocated impact per product was calculated to 11.4 and 12.4 ecopoints per ton of roast chicken and 35.2 and 33.4 ecopoints per

ton of breast fillet for free-range and conventional production respectively. The detailed life cycle analysis of Inghams' chicken production preformed here has shed light on which are the three environmental hot spots in the life cycle, namely feed ingredient production, broiler farm operation and meat processing plants. Feed ingredient production show significant improvement poten- tial; with environmental impact intensity ranging from 0.4 to 0.8 ecopoints per ton for wheat and grain co-product millrun to 8.4 ecopoints per ton from production of canola seed. The study has not successfully integrated and connected with other methods to address the broad range of challenges facing the agriculture and food industry such as exposures to pesticides, soil function impairment, biodiversity losses, and invasive GM crops. Several LCA studies of poultry production and products have been published. The most common unit for comparison is the calculated life-cycle global warming potential, typically assessed from cradle to farm gate or retail. Often sufficiently detailed information of the assumptions behind meat processing and allo- cation approach is missing in cradle to retail or consumer studies. A consistent and level playing field methodology must be established for the food sector in order for LCA to provide meaningful, reliable and comprehensive input into businesses decision-making.
